tasks.prompt:
Warm-up: Distinguish readiness probes from liveness probes. For each of the following behaviors, state whether it is caused by a failing readiness probe, a failing liveness probe, both, or neither — and explain what Kubernetes does in each case.

(a) A Pod is removed from the Service's load-balancing rotation but continues running.
(b) A Pod is restarted by Kubernetes.
(c) A rolling update is paused because a new Pod never becomes healthy.
(d) A Pod that is deadlocked (but still running) is automatically recovered.
(e) A slow-starting container is killed before its app finishes booting.
tasks.starter:
# For each behavior, state: readiness / liveness / both / neither
# and describe what Kubernetes does.

# (a) Pod removed from Service rotation, keeps running:
# Probe type: ________
# What Kubernetes does: ________

# (b) Pod is restarted by Kubernetes:
# Probe type: ________
# What Kubernetes does: ________

# (c) Rolling update paused because new Pod never becomes healthy:
# Probe type: ________
# What Kubernetes does: ________

# (d) Deadlocked (running but unresponsive) Pod is auto-recovered:
# Probe type: ________
# What Kubernetes does: ________

# (e) Slow-starting container killed before app finishes booting:
# Probe type: ________
# What Kubernetes does: ________
tasks.solution:
# (a) Pod removed from Service rotation, keeps running:
# Probe type: Readiness
# What Kubernetes does: Removes the Pod's IP from the Service's Endpoints list
#                       so new requests stop going to it; the Pod itself stays running.

# (b) Pod is restarted by Kubernetes:
# Probe type: Liveness
# What Kubernetes does: Kills the container (according to the Pod's restartPolicy)
#                       and starts a new container instance in the same Pod.

# (c) Rolling update paused because new Pod never becomes healthy:
# Probe type: Readiness
# What Kubernetes does: Blocks retirement of old Pods (maxUnavailable prevents
#                       taking them down while the new Pod isn't ready); the rollout
#                       stalls and 'kubectl rollout status' reports it as stuck.

# (d) Deadlocked (running but unresponsive) Pod is auto-recovered:
# Probe type: Liveness
# What Kubernetes does: The liveness HTTP/TCP check fails (app is alive as a process
#                       but not responding); Kubernetes kills and restarts the container.

# (e) Slow-starting container killed before app finishes booting:
# Probe type: Liveness (misconfigured — initialDelaySeconds too short)
# What Kubernetes does: The liveness probe fires before the app is ready, fails,
#                       and Kubernetes kills the container in a restart loop.
#                       The fix is a startup probe or a longer initialDelaySeconds.
tasks.explanation: The core rule: readiness controls whether traffic is sent (Pod in or out of Service), liveness controls whether the container is restarted. They operate independently. A failing readiness probe alone never kills a Pod — it just parks it out of rotation. A failing liveness probe always kills and restarts the container. Scenario (e) is the classic misconfiguration: setting initialDelaySeconds too short on the liveness probe causes Kubernetes to probe a still-booting container, fail, restart it, and loop. The startup probe was introduced precisely to hold off both liveness and readiness probes until the app signals 'I have finished starting'.
hints: The mnemonic from the lecture: readiness = traffic gate; liveness = restart trigger. Print this somewhere visible while debugging probe issues.
'kubectl describe pod <name>' shows probe failures in the Events section with messages like 'Liveness probe failed: HTTP probe failed with statuscode: 500'.
Use 'kubectl get endpoints <service-name>' to check whether a Pod's IP is currently in the Service's rotation — if missing, readiness probe failed.

tasks.prompt:
Core practice A — Add readiness and liveness probes to a Deployment manifest. The application exposes:
- GET /healthz on port 3000 → responds 200 when the process is alive
- GET /ready on port 3000 → responds 200 when connected to its database

The app takes about 8 seconds to start. Configure the probes so: liveness starts checking after 10 seconds (every 10 seconds), readiness starts checking after 5 seconds (every 5 seconds). Add both probes to the existing container spec below.
tasks.starter:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: my-app
          image: 111122223333.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-app:a1b9f3c
          ports:
            - containerPort: 3000
          # ADD livenessProbe here
          # ADD readinessProbe here
tasks.solution:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: my-app
          image: 111122223333.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-app:a1b9f3c
          ports:
            - containerPort: 3000
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 3000
            initialDelaySeconds: 10
            periodSeconds: 10
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /ready
              port: 3000
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 5
tasks.explanation: The probes use 'httpGet' — Kubernetes sends an HTTP GET to the specified path and port; a 2xx or 3xx response is success, anything else is failure. 'initialDelaySeconds' gives the container time to boot before the first probe fires — set it slightly longer than the typical startup time to avoid false failures during initial boot. 'periodSeconds' is how often to repeat. The readiness probe uses '/ready' (which checks database connectivity) with a shorter initialDelaySeconds because readiness failure only removes the Pod from the Service — it does not kill it — so it is safe to start checking earlier. The liveness probe uses '/healthz' with a longer initial delay because a liveness failure causes a restart, which is more disruptive.
hints: If your app does not expose a dedicated health endpoint, a TCP probe checks if the port is open: tcpSocket: { port: 3000 } — this is weaker but better than no probe.
Add 'failureThreshold: 3' (default is 3) to tolerate brief failures before triggering action — useful for transient network hiccups.
After applying, observe the probe results: kubectl describe pod <pod-name> and look at the 'Conditions' and 'Events' sections.

tasks.prompt: Core practice B — Configure rolling update parameters and practice the rollout commands. Add 'maxUnavailable: 1' and 'maxSurge: 1' to the Deployment's strategy. Then write the sequence of kubectl commands to: (1) trigger a new rollout by updating the image to tag 'b2c3d4e', (2) watch the rollout live, (3) pause the rollout, (4) resume it, and (5) view the rollout history.
tasks.starter:
# Part 1: Add rolling update strategy to the Deployment spec
# (Add under spec:, before replicas:)
spec:
  strategy:
    type: ________
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: ________
      maxSurge: ________
  replicas: 3
  ...

# Part 2: Write the kubectl commands

# (1) Update image to tag 'b2c3d4e'
kubectl set image ________

# (2) Watch the rollout live (block until done or failed)
kubectl rollout ________

# (3) Pause the rollout mid-way
kubectl rollout ________

# (4) Resume the rollout
kubectl rollout ________

# (5) View rollout history
kubectl rollout ________
tasks.solution:
# Part 1: Rolling update strategy
spec:
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1
      maxSurge: 1
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
  ...

# Part 2: kubectl commands

# (1) Update image to tag 'b2c3d4e'
kubectl set image deployment/my-app \
  my-app=111122223333.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-app:b2c3d4e

# (2) Watch the rollout live (block until done or failed)
kubectl rollout status deployment/my-app

# (3) Pause the rollout mid-way
kubectl rollout pause deployment/my-app

# (4) Resume the rollout
kubectl rollout resume deployment/my-app

# (5) View rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/my-app
tasks.explanation: With replicas=3, maxUnavailable=1, maxSurge=1: Kubernetes will never let more than 1 Pod be below desired count, and never spin up more than 4 total (3+1). The rollout proceeds one Pod at a time — retire one old Pod, spin up one new Pod, wait for it to pass readiness, repeat. 'pause' is useful when you spot a problem (e.g., metrics looking bad) midway through a rollout — it freezes the update at the current state, leaving some old and some new Pods running. 'resume' continues from where it stopped. 'rollout history' shows all revisions — each time you change the Pod template, Kubernetes records a new revision that you can roll back to.
hints: After 'kubectl rollout pause', 'kubectl get pods' will show a mix of old and new Pods — the rollout is frozen at that split.
To annotate a rollout with a change reason (useful in history): kubectl annotate deployment/my-app kubernetes.io/change-cause='update to tag b2c3d4e' — this shows in rollout history.
maxUnavailable and maxSurge can be percentages (e.g., '25%') or absolute integers. Percentages are rounded down for maxUnavailable and up for maxSurge.

tasks.prompt: Applied challenge — A colleague deployed a broken image that causes all new Pods to crash immediately ('CrashLoopBackOff'). The deployment has 3 replicas, maxUnavailable=1, maxSurge=1, and has readiness probes configured. Explain what state the cluster is in right now (how many old vs. new Pods), then write the commands to: (1) verify the stuck rollout, (2) check why the new Pods are failing, and (3) roll back to revision 2 specifically.
tasks.starter:
# Step 0: Explain the cluster state
# With maxUnavailable=1 and readiness probes failing on new Pods,
# how many old Pods are still running? How many new Pods are in CrashLoopBackOff?
# Explanation: ________

# Step 1: Verify the rollout is stuck
kubectl ________

# Step 2: Find out why new Pods are crashing
kubectl get pods
# (pick a crashing pod name and inspect it)
kubectl ________
kubectl logs ________ --previous

# Step 3: Roll back to revision 2 specifically
kubectl ________
tasks.solution:
# Step 0: Cluster state explanation
# With maxUnavailable=1 and the readiness probe failing on new Pods:
# - Kubernetes retired at most 1 old Pod (maxUnavailable=1 permits taking 1 down)
# - It started 1 new Pod (maxSurge=1) which is CrashLoopBackOff and never passes readiness
# - Because the new Pod never becomes ready, RBAC blocks retiring more old Pods
# - Result: 2 old Pods still running (healthy, serving traffic) +
#           1 new Pod in CrashLoopBackOff + possibly 1 that was terminated
# Users are still served by the 2 remaining old Pods — zero user impact so far.

# Step 1: Verify the rollout is stuck
kubectl rollout status deployment/my-app
# Expected output: 'Waiting for deployment "my-app" rollout to finish:
#   1 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...'

# Step 2: Find out why new Pods are crashing
kubectl get pods
# Identify pods in CrashLoopBackOff, e.g.: my-app-7d9f8b-xk9mn
kubectl describe pod my-app-7d9f8b-xk9mn
# Look at Events: exit codes, OOM, missing env vars, bad image, etc.
kubectl logs my-app-7d9f8b-xk9mn --previous
# '--previous' fetches logs from the last crashed container instance

# Step 3: Roll back to revision 2 specifically
kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-app --to-revision=2
# Monitor recovery:
kubectl rollout status deployment/my-app
tasks.explanation: This scenario illustrates exactly why rolling updates + readiness probes are safe: the new crashing Pods never pass their readiness check, so maxUnavailable prevents Kubernetes from retiring more old Pods. At most 1 old Pod is retired and 1 new Pod is added (maxSurge=1), leaving 2 healthy old Pods serving traffic — users never notice. The '--previous' flag on 'kubectl logs' retrieves logs from the last terminated container, which is essential when a Pod keeps restarting (current logs may be empty if the crash happens very quickly). Rolling back to a specific revision ('--to-revision=2') is useful when you want to skip the immediately preceding revision (which might also be bad) and go to a known-good state.
hints: 'kubectl rollout history deployment/my-app' shows all revision numbers. Use '--revision=<N>' to see the details of a specific revision: kubectl rollout history deployment/my-app --revision=2
CrashLoopBackOff usually means the container starts and immediately exits non-zero. Check exit code in 'kubectl describe pod' under 'Last State: Terminated: Exit Code'.
After the rollback completes, 'kubectl get pods' should show all 3 Pods in Running/Ready state with the old image tag.
